medications.indication_rationale: For acute tension-type headache, used sparingly to avoid medication-overuse headache; source used paracetamol 1000mg + ibuprofen 400mg then regular paracetamol PRN.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000mg every 6 hours PRN ± ibuprofen 400mg with food; limit acute analgesic use to <2–3 days/week (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: opioids / frequent combination analgesics for tension headache
medications.indication_rationale: Opioids and frequent combination analgesics are not recommended (poor evidence; medication-overuse headache/dependence).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Address stress/posture: gentle neck/shoulder stretches every couple of hours, screen breaks, ergonomic workstation, heat to the neck/shoulders, relaxation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces pericranial muscle tension driving the headache and the recurrence risk.

patient_education_points.point: This is a tension-type headache — a benign 'muscle tension' headache brought on by stress and posture, not a sign of anything dangerous based on the questions we've been through. It usually settles within a day or two.
Use simple pain relief sparingly (not most days, to avoid rebound headaches), do regular neck/shoulder stretches and screen breaks, and manage stress. Warm showers/heat on the shoulders help.

Call 000 immediately for a sudden 'thunderclap' worst-ever headache, weakness/numbness, slurred speech, vision loss, or a fever with a stiff neck. See a doctor promptly for headaches worse on waking/lying down, or (if over 50) jaw pain on chewing or scalp tenderness.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Frequency; preventive strategies; reconsider red flags/medication-overuse.

must_include_items: Screen secondary-headache red flags with a thunderclap/meningism/focal-deficit safety-net
Simple analgesia used sparingly (avoid medication-overuse headache)
errors_of_commission: Reassuring a new severe headache without excluding thunderclap/GCA
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a secondary-headache red-flag screen with safety-net, sparing simple analgesia, and trigger management.
